data.title: Zillow: AI Isn't A Credible Threat So I'm Buying The Plunge
data.description:
Zillow (ZG) shares have been decimated with shares have declining 45% over the past year and were down 17% alone on Wednesday following earnings (and likely caught up in the real estate services AI fear plunge). I last wrote about Zillow in late 2023. At that time, shares were under attack due to changes in the industry commission structure, a competitive onslaught from Homes.com (discussed below) and a dismal housing market. Despite these challenges, Zillow continued to grow revenue, EBITDA, and free cash flow - shares more than doubled before giving back most of their gains in the recent plunge.

Today the fear surrounding Zillow includes AI disruption, private listing networks, as well as a still-weak housing market. As I discuss below, I doubt that AI or private listing networks will have much of a negative impact on Zillow. Moreover, I see the eventual rebound of the housing market as a catalyst for Zillow to achieve its long-term financial objectives. Looking out 3-4 years, I expect that shares will double (or better) from today's depressed levels.

Assessing the AI Threat to Zillow

Even if AI instantly replicated Zillow's platform & data, it would almost certainly struggle to garner mass consumer attention. Consider CoStar's (CSGP) Homes.com has spent ~$3 billion over the past three years in an effort to challenge Zillow's dominance as the leading US housing portal. As shown above, despite spending billions to fund a near constant barrage of marketing, Homes.com has made very little headway in gaining market share (interestingly, activist investors are pushing CoStar to abandon Homes.com). The behavior of consumers here is instructive as rather than Googling 'houses for sale in Lincoln Park', the vast majority of times (~80%), they go directly to Zillow. This makes Zillow incredibly hard to displace - as evidenced by the foundering of Homes.com.

Zillow has built its consumer brand over a 20 year period and has essentially become the default option for home seekers in the US. While AI may eventually be able to build a housing super app and populate it with current data, it is far from clear how any such app would displace Zillow in the mind of consumers.

Private Listing Networks

Last year, Compass (COMP) sought to take more control of its listings through a multiphase approach to some (estimated 20-25%) listings, including:

  • Private Exclusive (aka pocket listing) - only the broker knows the property is for sale - the broker contacts prospective buyers in his/her network to gauge interest and receive feedback on price. This is not a new practice.
  • Compass puts the property on its website as 'Coming Soon' but the property is not officially listed on the MLS (multiple listing service) or Zillow.
  • Property is officially 'listed' on the MLS/Zillow.

The first two phases increase the chances that Compass can 'double end' (get commissions from both buyer & seller) the transaction. This would effectively lock Zillow out of the deal. This threat has been amplified by Compass acquiring Anywhere real estate which give the combined group an estimated ~18% share of brokerage in the US residential real estate business.

In response, on June 30, 2025, Zillow banned from its site/portal any listing which has been listed for more than 24 hours on a private network website. Of course Compass sued though its motion for a preliminary injunction on the ban was denied. Note that in reporting 4Q25 results, Zillow cited higher legal costs as a headwind (100 bps) to expected 2026 EBITDA margins.

From a home seller perspective, there are two potential disadvantages with Compass's phased approach. First, the phased approach is likely to increase the amount of time needed to sell a property. Second, the phased approach could negatively impact the price received if the property isn't broadly marketed to the widest pool of potential buyers. Time to close and price received are the main criteria for home sellers. To secure listings, I expect most brokers will shun the phased listing approach.

From a buyer perspective, the phased approach reduces transparency by understating the number of 'days on market' as the clock doesn't start until it hits the MLS.

While the initial legal ruling was favorable to Zillow, even if it ultimately is forced to rescind the ban, I don't believe private listing networks will have a material negative impact on the business as I think home sellers will insist their agents market their homes as broadly as possible to ensure a reasonably quick sale at the best possible price.

Significant Upside when Housing Transactions pick up

As shown above, US housing transactions are at a cyclical low driven by the rise in mortgage rates starting in 2022 which had the dual effect of reducing affordability (depressing the buyer pool) and creating a lock-in effect on the part of would-be sellers (why give up a 3-4% mortgage). When transactions eventually pick up, Zillow should be a prime beneficiary. As shown below, in a normalized (~6 million annual transactions) market, Zillow believes it can generate multiples of its current EBITDA/free cash flow.

As Zillow continues to expand its Enhanced markets program (fka Zillow Flex), improves cross-sale of mortgage products, and continues to grow its rentals business, Zillow management believes it can generate $2.25 billion of EBITDA in a more normalized housing market (versus $750-800 million expected for 2026).

Assuming that over the next four years Zillow merely doubles its EBITDA to $1.5 billion and trades at a 15x EBITDA multiple (compared with a range of 12-30x EBITDA over the past 5 years), I arrive at a share price of $100 per share (over 100% upside). While a doubling of EBITDA may sound aggressive, consider that Zillow is on track to double its EBITDA between 2023-2026, largely through its own initiative (has been a tough housing market the whole time). Said differently, Zillow expects to produce nearly the same level of EBITDA in 2026 as it did in 2021 when the housing market was strong (6.1 million transactions).

Risks

If high mortgage rates persist, it could slow the housing market recovery and dampen Zillow's results. That said, as discussed above, Zillow has experienced strong growth even in a high mortgage rate environment over the past few years.

If Zillow loses the Compass suit, the company could lose some market share, though as I discuss above, I don't expect this to have a material negative effect.

Conclusion

I believe that the fear surrounding Zillow's business is misplaced and ultimately expect that the market will recognize this. With a leading consumer brand, dominant market position, and strong balance sheet I see Zillow as a long-term winner which will benefit from an eventual rebound in the US housing market.

data.title: FormFactor's Margin Pivot Looks Real And HBM Is The Fuel
data.description:
Investment Thesis

First and foremost, let me say that I think FormFactor, Inc. (NASDAQ:FORM) is one of the picks and shovel names that looks boring right until it doesn't. The market is fixated on the shiny end of AI, like GPUs and giant data center builds. Meanwhile, the real constraint in advanced packaging is often yield, test time, and how many goods you can push through the line without shipping defects. And obviously, that pushes value into probe cards and test hardware.

Here's the part that matters most to me. Management is explicitly describing a world where HBM stack complexity keeps rising (such as HBM3E, HBM4 Ramp, and work towards HBM5). This translates into higher test intensity and harder specs. And that is not marketing fluff. It is the economics of advanced packaging where one bad chiplet can kill the whole stack.

Now, a good story does not automatically mean a good stock. But what interests me with FORM is that the company is also showing tangible operational improvement. The Q4 2025 earnings update points to an expanding non-GAAP gross margin and continued strength into Q1 2026. In my opinion, that combination, demand together with strategic execution, is what I would say keeps a "good company" from becoming a "good but overpriced" situation.

I am rating FormFactor a Buy. And this is not because the stock is cheap today. Actually, it is not. But I am buying the idea that earnings power is rising faster than many investors are modeling. And that the next few quarters can keep surprising on margin while the revenue line stays firm.

Business Overview

FormFactor sits in semiconductor test and measurement across the IC lifecycle, from early characterization and debug to qualification and high-volume production test. The business is reported in two segments. First are the Probe cards, which include probe cards and analytical probes. Second are systems, which are basically probe stations plus thermal and cryogenic systems. What I believe many people miss is that probe cards are not generic parts that you swap in like printer ink. For cutting-edge memory and logic, they're engineered interfaces that need to stay stable, hit speed targets, and perform across thermal extremes. FormFactor's smartMatrix line is well positioned for advanced DRAM and HBM use cases, with an emphasis on high parallelism and production demands.

Growth Drivers

Now, having gone through the business model and investment thesis around FORM, I would not want to pretend that catalysts are magic. They are only useful if they change numbers. And when I look at FormFactor, I can see three that can.

The first one is a capacity with a lower structural cost. The company bought Farmers Branch, a Texas manufacturing site, for $55Mn with 50,000 square feet of clean room space. This is what I would call a scarce but fit-for-purpose. If this ramps up on time and at the expected cost, then it becomes a real lever for both revenue capacity and margin.

Then the second is CPO and Silicon photonics expansion. FormFactor acquired Keystone Photonics in December 2025, explicitly positioning it as an optical probing technology expansion for silicon photonics. This is in addition to co-packaged optics wafer testing. So why does this matter? It matters because AI data centers are running into bandwidth and power constraints, and optical interconnect is one of the next 'big step' conversations for the rack. Even external market work points to a high-growth SiPh backdrop. MarketsandMarkets, for instance, projects the silicon photonics market growing from $2.65Bn to $9.65Bn. This is around a 29.5% CAGR.

Then the third catalyst is milestone visibility. I say this because management has pointed investors to an Analyst Day, which has been signaled for May 2026. And this is purely to discuss an updated target model and strategy. I believe, if that event lays out a credible path from "better quarter" to "higher through-cycle earnings power," then I definitely believe that is a re-rating moment for FORM.

Valuation

I will be honest, FORM isn't trading like a neglected small cap. And I say this because when I look at FormFactor's FWD Non-GAAP P/E, I note that the company is trading at 50.31x and 32x on a FWD EV/EBITDA basis as shown below. Now, those are high numbers. And having said that, I would say that the debate is not about whether FORM is cheap. The debate is, "Does the earnings power curve bend up fast enough to make the multiple look reasonable in hindsight?"

And here's how I frame it. Against a peer set that lives in test, probe cards, and semi equipment, I would say that FORM's FWD P/E is in line with Teradyne (TER). TER is trading at a FWD Non-GAAP P/E of about 48x and above Advantest (ATEYY), which is about 43x. Yet it is below a probe card peer like Technoprobe, which sits at 68x. On an EV/EBITDA basis, FORM looks richer than the same peer set. TER sits at 57x while ATEYY sits at 40x and Technoprobe at around 57x. This means that FORM's 72x is quite a premium.

Nevertheless, a premium multiple can be fine if two things stay in tune. Revenue stays supported by real end demand, and gross margin expansion is structural. And not a one-off. The Q4 2025 earnings report supports that both claims are at least directional. They talk about a record 2025 revenue, and they emphasize a continued gross margin improvement path.

So, I'm not pitching a multiple expansion trade. I am pitching a multiple compression through an earnings growth story. If they keep taking gross margin up while maintaining strong demand in advanced packaging and high-performance compute, then the denominator can do the heavy lifting.

FormFactor's Key Risks

The biggest risk in one line is if the AI or HBM memory cycle cools sharply, then FORM can lose volume and mix at the same time. Secondly, execution risk on Farmers Branch is also a key risk. But as a secondary risk. The company is convinced that this asset is strategic and that it expects meaningful future cash expenditures around it. If the timeline slips or costs inflate, then that delays both capacity and margin benefits. And the market won't be patient, especially at a premium multiple.

Final Thoughts

At the end of the day, I like FORM because the company is standing in the middle of a very specific bottleneck. Advanced packaging, HBM ramps, and AI Infrastructure are all pushing test complexity up. That drives demand for better probe cards and better test solutions.

I also like that management is talking realistically about margins. They are not promising magic. They are talking about cycle times, yields, and cost discipline. And that's exactly the kind of boring work that creates durable earnings power. So, yes, the valuation is demanding at a premium. But I believe the earnings power is moving up too, and the catalyst calendar is real. That is why I believe FormFactor warrants a Buy rating.

data.title: Main Street Capital: Quality Is Not The Question, Valuation Is
data.description:
Introduction: Execution is fine, Valuation isn’t

Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN) has always been a quality company. Its NAV per share has been growing for 13 consecutive quarters. Q4 2025 NAV is estimated to be around $33.29–$33.37, up roughly 1.5–1.8% quarter over quarter. Full-year annualized return estimates on equity exceed 17%, according to management. One more positive: distributable net investment income easily covers the regular dividend.

On the other side of this story, MAIN shares trade around $61–62, so roughly 1.8-1.9x NAV. That’s around 40-50% higher than the BDC sector median and also well above MAIN’s own long-term average price-to-book multiple. In other words, this is premium pricing for a quality company that leaves no margin of error in execution.

So, the market assumes that MAIN will be able to reliably deliver income, just not a lot of price appreciation. Returns will come mostly from dividends, and even modest multiple compressions will hurt.

Now, MAIN’s estimated forward dividend yield comes to 6.9-7.0%, with an annual payout of ~$4.30 per share. This goes to support the yield and is paid monthly. Compared to the broad financial sector, this yield is attractive enough. However, it is below MAIN’s own historical average. This means today’s investors are willing to pay more for a lower yield only because it has proven to be durable.

Earnings estimates, on the other hand, are flat to slightly negative overall, according to street consensus. Estimated EPS growth through 2027 is close to zero. That again means that future returns are likely primarily dividend-driven.

This brings us to the MAIN problem: this BDC can keep on performing well. It can originate first-lien loans, realize profits, issue equity at a premium to NAV, and pay dependable monthly dividends. It can do all these positives—but they are all baked into its current price. So it will only be able to deliver mid-single-digit total returns if valuation multiples remain stable, lower if they compress modestly.

Main Street is undoubtedly a quality business. The purpose of this article is not to challenge that. I just want to discuss how we should rate MAIN, trading as it is at nearly 2x NAV.

What Actually Drives MAIN’s Premium Valuation

Main Street has a number of structural qualities that justify premium pricing.

First, it is internally managed, which immediately sets it apart from most BDCs. According to a recent SEC document, BDCs that are “managed by an investment advisor”—meaning externally managed BDCs—typically charge “1.5%-2% of the fund’s gross assets annually, plus certain incentive fees generally up to 20% of any profits earned.” MAIN charges (to be very precise, its operating expenses minus interests) around 1.3% of average assets—materially lower than those of externally managed BDCs. This savings goes directly into your net investment income and, over time, into NAV per share.

The second factor is portfolio composition. MAIN deals mostly in the lower middle market. These are companies with $10–150 million in revenue and $3–20 million in EBITDA. MAIN typically combines first-lien debt with a meaningful equity stake in its target companies. Its homework is strong, so those equity positions often make strong realized gains that strengthen NAV. That, in turn, supports dividend growth and also periodic supplemental dividends. This strategy has resulted in substantial cumulative NAV growth and net realized gains above $170 million. Since its IPO, MAIN has grown NAV per share from roughly $15 to over $33, representing cumulative NAV growth of approximately 120%, excluding dividends. To take one example, Ares Capital (ARCC) has seen NAV growth from ~$15 in 2004 to ~$20 in 2025. For Capital Southwest (CSWC) as well—which became a BDC in 2015—NAV has only grown around 11% in these 10 years. Both peers have preserved NAV, but MAIN has compounded it.

Main Street’s third advantage is its flexible balance sheet. It has low leverage; it is rated investment-grade, so it can get long-duration, low-cost SBIC financing. This helps Main Street to continue to originate new loans even when markets are stressed. It does not need to sell assets or raise capital on unfavorable terms to do that. So, if there’s a valuation change, it is primarily due to fundamentals, not liquidity pressure. This has kept its NAV volatility low.

Finally, Main Street’s shares trade well above book value. Therefore, when it issues fresh equity, it can generally price them at a premium. Because these are priced well above NAV, these new shares are accretive to NAV, not dilutive. Once capital markets free up, MAIN can raise equity, buy assets that generate income, and still increase per-share value.

These are the reasons Main Street is able to almost always trade above NAV. These four structural features see to it. However, when valuation is too high, these same features merely act as shock absorbers. They do not help generate higher returns.

Valuation: Breaking Down Forward Returns

We can look at MAIN’s expected total return using three components. These are a) dividend yield, b) NAV growth, and c) change in valuation multiple.

Let’s start with income, so we have MAIN’s forward dividend yield at ~7%. If there’s no reinvestment and no change in price, even then, total return will not go below this number.

Take NAV growth now. It has compounded at ~4-4.5% annually since about 2007. However, valuations were lower; the platform grew faster in those early days. At today’s scale and premium, a 2-3% assumption going forward isn’t conservative. If we assume that, we get a baseline expected return of roughly 9–10% before considering valuation changes.

Once you consider valuation changes, though, the math breaks apart. MAIN already trades at close to 2x NAV, much higher than the sector range of 1.2–1.4x. Even if that premium simply holds steady, total returns are just the dividend plus modest NAV growth—that's the cap. If we—quite legitimately—assume that multiples compress even modestly—say from ~1.9x to ~1.6x over the next few years—that alone would subtract 2-3% per year from total returns. That would offset most or all of the NAV growth.

Put another way, for MAIN, a stable multiple implies mid- to high-single-digit total returns. A modest compression implies returns fall back toward the dividend. A material compression would—despite solid execution—produce negative total returns.

This is, like I noted before, a valuation problem. The current high valuation may be well justified, but it leaves no room for error. Main Street does not have to fail operationally to produce poor returns. It can perform well, but if the premium just moves towards the sector median even slightly, investors will be looking at disappointment.

Bottom line: Why this supports a Hold, not a Buy

At current pricing, investors are effectively pricing in three things: a) sustained low volatility, b) uninterrupted dividends, and c) continued access to accretive equity issuance.

Those assumptions may well be true, but there’s little excess return to justify buying MAIN now. There’s no margin of safety.

On the other hand, purely based on that very strong business quality alone, MAIN is not a sell. I continue to rate MAIN a Hold: attractive as a reliable income instrument for existing shareholders, unattractive for new or incremental buying unless valuation improves.

data.title: January CPI Report: Lower Headline Inflation As Gas Prices Provide Tailwind
data.description:
This week treated market observers with a double dose of key macro data courtesy of the short-lived government shutdown. A better-than-expected jobs report earlier in the week did little to lift equities out of their AI-driven slump. To end the week on this Friday the 13th, market observers were likely just looking to head into the weekend without any scares. The January CPI release fortunately lived up to those wishes.

Leading into the release, all three indexes have been slammed by worries over possible AI-related disruptions. The Nasdaq Composite (NDX) has been the worst impacted, with everything from software companies, payment processors, and chip companies, themselves, selling off. The Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) and S&P 500 (SP500, SPX) have also slipped in recent days, with the two indexes closing 1.3% and 1.5% lower on Thursday.

Today’s CPI release, which landed roughly in line with expectations, shouldn’t alter the current trajectory of the Fed’s policy, nor should it significantly swing stocks out of their current slump, in my view. Here’s what else to know about the January CPI release.

Where Did Prices Increase in January?

The headline rate rose 2.4% in January, both slightly below expectations and down slightly from the reading in December.

While consumers are still being hit with the double whammy of slowing job growth and still-high prices, one line item that they can continue to rejoice in is gasoline. During the month, the gasoline index declined by a rather significant 3.2% on the month.

This accelerated the trend seen over the last several years, with gas prices steadily falling below the $3/gallon mark. In its latest reporting, the AAA noted that gas prices were heading higher in advance of the holiday weekend, though prices are still lower YOY and still average below $3/gallon. The rise could impact February’s CPI reading, though I believe the index will remain tame.

The declines in the gasoline were offset somewhat by the continued rise in the food index. After rising 0.7% in December, the index added another 0.2% in February, led by a 0.2% rise in the food at home index and a 0.1% rise in the food away from home index. The increases in groceries were driven primarily by a 1.2% increase in the cereals and bakery index.

What Core Categories Increased in January?

The core categories came in right around expectations, with a rise of 2.5% YOY.

Consumers continued to see an opportunity within used vehicles, with the index declining 1.8% on the month, following a 0.9% decrease in the month prior. This was offset slightly by a 0.1% increase in the new index. For buyers seeking a relatively affordable option, used definitely appears to be the way to go.

Medical care commodities also let up some steam in January, with the index declining 0.1% on the month. Granted, the index is still up 0.3% over the last twelve.

Likewise, the familiar categories of shelter and insurance continue to be the primary drivers of the stickiness in the core index, and I expect this to continue in the months ahead.

What Are the Fed's Policy Implications of the January CPI Report?

Much like the January jobs report earlier in the week, I don’t believe today’s CPI print materially shifts the near-term policy outlook.

Inflation was rather uneventful on both a headline and core basis, and while inflation was lower, it was not so in a way that dramatically changed the broader narrative.

Policymakers have made it clear in recent months that they are looking at trends over time, and increasingly, their attention has tilted toward signs of slowing in the labor market rather than reacting to any single inflation release.

Market expectations reflect that steadiness. Observers remain firmly anchored to the view that the Fed will hold rates steady at the March meeting, with probabilities hovering around 90%. That conviction is unlikely to change based on today’s data.

Looking further out, the June meeting continues to carry roughly a 50% probability of a rate cut, particularly as Chairman Jerome Powell’s term concludes in late May.

While the data will likely not force the Fed’s hand anytime soon, it’s worth mentioning that inflation remains a political pain point. Consumers are still feeling elevated shelter, insurance, and everyday living costs.

Even if the trajectory is improving incrementally, the level of prices continues to shape sentiment, and that dynamic won’t be lost on policymakers navigating both economic and political crosscurrents, in my view.

Final Takeaways of the January CPI Report

I believe January’s CPI report offered a modest sigh of relief. Headline inflation cooled more than expected, aided in large part by the continued decline in gasoline prices, while core inflation edged lower as well.

After a busy week filled with earnings headlines, AI-driven volatility, and a better-than-expected jobs report that failed to meaningfully lift sentiment, today’s data likely allows investors to head into the weekend at relative ease. Sometimes, uneventful is exactly what the market needs.

That said, I don’t believe this report changes the broader investment landscape. Much like my overarching views of the labor data that I covered earlier this week, inflation is easing but not in a way that meaningfully alters the trajectory of policy or growth.

Shelter and insurance remain sticky, job growth is slowing, and market leadership continues to narrow amid heightened volatility, particularly within tech and AI-linked names. In my view, this combination continues to call for a defensive posture.

Lower inflation is welcome, but it does not eliminate the risks tied to slowing growth, elevated valuations, and policy uncertainty. As I’ve noted previously in my 2026 market outlook article, selectivity, not momentum, is likely to define the investment landscape ahead.

data.title: American Electric Power: Strong Q4 Earnings Confirm Data Centers Are A Catalyst
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered American Electric Power Company, Inc. (AEP), I issued a Buy rating due to the strong earnings and several growth initiatives that management highlighted. I believed that these growth initiatives could lead to higher earnings for the business. Since my last coverage, the share price has risen steadily and has outpaced the S&P 500 (SP500).

I wanted to revisit the business after its most recent Q4 earnings because I believe the growth catalysts have become more significant over the last quarter. AEP can now provide exposure to the growth of the AI markets, by servicing the growing demand for AI data centers.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that AEP has provided a total return of nearly 24%. This total return includes all dividends that were paid out to shareholders. AEP now offers investors a starting dividend yield of about 3.1%, while offering a strong potential for continued dividend growth in the future. AEP now trades at all-time highs, but I don't think this is a reason for investors to stop accumulating. I believe in letting your winners run, and I believe that AEP is a clear winner for investors looking for a straightforward buy-and-hold dividend growth position.

While I am bullish on the outlook of AEP, there are some risks to consider. For instance, the shift in growth initiatives that have some exposure to AI can expose AEP's stock price to higher than usual levels of volatility. The AI sector is driven by sentiment and so far the sector's performance for 2026 has been a bit disappointing. So AEP having some direct exposure to the growth of AI can introduce a new level of price sensitivity that isn't typical for the stock. So let's start by reviewing the highlights of the most recent earnings report.

Q4 Earnings Confirms More Growth Ahead

AEP is one of the largest electric utilities companies in the U.S., and its business spans across 11 states. The strength of the business lies within its ownership of its actual infrastructure assets. AEP owns high voltage lines across the U.S. and when we think of a business like this, it is more common to think about the retail customers they service. However, AEP's growth potential is becoming more attractive as they begin to utilize their infrastructure to service the institutional clients that want to utilize their assets to service the growing demand for data centers.

AEP recently reported their Q4 earnings, and I believe the results were exceptional. Most notably, the business reported earnings per share of $1.19, which beat expectations by $0.05 per share. Total operating EPS amounted to $5.97 per share, with the vertically integrated utilities segment making up the biggest bulk of earnings.

Total Operating EPS Breakdown (EPS Contribution)

  • Vertically Integrated Utilities: $2.83 per share
  • Transmission & Distribution Utilities: $1.61 per share
  • AEP Transmission Holdco: $1.51 per share
  • Generation & Marketing: $0.57 per share
  • Corporate & Other: ($0.56) per share.

Additionally, revenue for the quarter amounted to $5.31B, which represented a year over year growth of 13.2%. After reviewing its report, I believe that the main contributors to the earnings growth were a combination between its load growth, favorable regulatory shifts, and healthy operating margins.

For instance, AEP was able to deliver over 200M megawatt hours of total system sales throughout 2025. As I previously stated, electric utilities companies are typically associated with their level of service to retail and residential clients. However, AEP's residential sales only saw a 2.9% growth, while the commercial and industrial segment experienced a 9.9% growth for the quarter. Most importantly, the sales volume increased by 7.5%, but the actual associated revenue increased by 8.3%, which highlights AEP's flexible minimum demand charges. This helps ensure that AEP gets paid even if the customer's usage shifts over a given period.

With these systems working together in AEP's favor, management highlighted its positive growth outlook over the next few years. Management believes that there will be a total annual expected shareholder return between 10% to 13% through 2030. This will be driven by a strategic capital plan that can boost earnings, alongside $5B to $8B of confirmed incremental projects through 2030.

Data Centers

One of the most impactful drivers of higher earnings will be the acceleration in demand from data centers. As AI continues to be a driving force throughout the tech sector, businesses continue to allocate massive amounts of capital towards capex investments with the purpose of building out their AI infrastructure. For instance, Grand View Research believes that the size of the AI data center market will increase to $60.5B by 2030. This represents an estimated CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 28.3% through 2030. In a single quarter, AEP was able to double their contracted load outlook from 28 gigawatts to 56 gigawatts of demand by 2030, which aligns with the expected growth of the sector.

We've already been able to experience how AEP is starting to capitalize on the sector's demand. For instance, AEP recently signed a $2.65B fuel cell deal with Bloom Energy (BE), which will help meet the needs of a new power generation facility in Wyoming. This deal helps meet the power needs for the AI data centers in the area. I suspect that we will see more deals similar like this over the next few years, considering the $72B capital plan in place. Management has already identified how this capital will be spread across each of its segments, as seen below.

So despite AEP currently earning a C- valuation grade, I believe that the accumulation of shares here is still reasonable. If management is able to execute on these future investments, while keeping its capital management strong, I believe that AEP will hit the higher end of its expected growth. The business still has some of the strongest growth metrics compared to peers in the sector. Furthermore, AEP's current growth is now outpacing its own five-year averages. So despite AEP trading at all-time highs, I believe that investors should not let anchoring bias stop them from accumulating a high quality business that has continued growth potential.

Dividend Growth

As of the latest declared quarterly dividend of $0.95 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 3.1%. Although the starting dividend yield is low, I love the fact that the business has a healthy dividend growth history. For instance, AEP has been able to raise dividends at a CAGR of 5.60% over the last ten years. AEP has been able to raise dividends for 16 consecutive years without interruption and with a payout ratio of 61.90%, I believe there is a strong probability that these dividend raises will continue through the next five years.

To be rewarded with a growing stream of dividend income, AEP requires an investor to remain patient. For instance, long-term investors that have held shares for the last ten years would now have a yield on cost of about ~6.1%. As time goes on and AEP continues to successfully execute its growth initiatives, the business is capable of providing a high yield that rivals some of the highest quality covered call ETFs out there.

Additionally, the dividend income received from AEP would be classified as qualified dividends, which have the most favorable tax consequences for investors. This provides flexibility in terms of which account types AEP can be held within. To demonstrate the strength of its dividend raises, I ran a back test of an initial $10,000 investment at the start of 2017. This calculation assumes that no additional capital was added after the initial $10K. It also assumes that all dividends received were reinvested back into the accumulation of shares. By remaining invested, investors would have been able to double their annual dividend income without ever adding any new capital.

2017 Dividend Income: $385

2025 Dividend Income: $799

Risks

While I am optimistic about the growth of AI and data centers, I am cautious about how this can impact AEP's stock price. Utilities are typically the place where investors go to seek stability over a longer holding period. However, this exposure to data centers means that AEP is now somewhat exposed to the volatility of the tech sector. If the broader market continues to sell off tech, AEP's share price could potentially retreat from its highs.

Another challenge can be the massive spending required for the new infrastructure to be built out. If demand continues to grow rapidly, the physical limitations and AEP's existing grids may become a problem, which opens up an opportunity for competitors to take business. Additionally, I have a concern about how the separation between AEP's residential clients and commercial clients will be maintained. There is always a risk that the cost of maintaining these commercial clients starts to negatively impact existing residential clients.

Lastly, I believe that interest rates continue to play a part in shaping the growth momentum of the sector. Although unlikely, a scenario where interest rates rise can cause some of its growth initiatives to be backlogged for future dates. AEP may suffer from a higher cost of debt and a higher interest expense can negatively impact operating margins.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that AEP remains a Buy today. Despite the stock trading at all-time highs, I believe that AEP's management has provided transparency into the outlook of the company. The business reported strong Q4 earnings and has been able to efficiently capture the growing demand from its commercial clients. As the growth of AI data centers is estimated to increase, I believe that AEP is well positioned to continue capitalizing on this growth. The dividend remains well supported and I believe that dividend growth can continue over the next few years.

data.title: TPG RE Finance Trust: High Yield Common And Preferred 8-10%
data.description:
With interest rates poised to decline in 2026, mortgage REITs should get a boost from the market, as their borrowing costs will also decline. The lower costs would help buttress them against prepayments.

Mortgage REITs generally offer high-yield dividends via using leverage to achieve net interest spreads. TPG RE Finance Trust, Inc. (TRTX) offers a common and preferred dividend, the TPG RE Finance Trust Preferred C shares (TRTX.PR.C), which, as of 2/10/26, yielded 10.71% and 8.40%, respectively:

Company Profile:

TPG RE Finance Trust, Inc. is a holding company that engages in the provision of commercial real estate finance services. It originates, acquires, and manages commercial mortgage loans and other commercial real estate-related debt instruments in North America. The company was founded on October 24, 2014, and is headquartered in New York, NY.

Holdings:

TRTX does 3 types of loans:

  1. Bridge Loans have limited deferred fundings, generally less than 10% of the total loan commitment, which fundings are commonly conditioned on the borrower’s satisfaction of certain collateral performance tests.
  2. Light Transitional Loans have deferred fundings ranging from 10% to 20% of the total loan commitment, which fundings are commonly conditioned on the borrower’s completion of specified improvements to the property or satisfaction of certain collateral performance tests.
  3. Moderate Transitional Loans have deferred fundings greater than 20% of the total loan commitment, with similar completion hurdles. As of 9/30/25, Bridge Loans remained the highest exposure, at 52.1%, up from 44.6% at 12/31/24. Moderate Transitional Loans declined to 25.4%, vs. 29.6% at 12/31/24, and Light Transitional Loans fell from 25.8% to 22.5%.

As of 9/30/25, TRTX had a $3.7B loan portfolio, with a weighted average yield of 7.75% and an average 3.36% credit spread. This is a floating loan portfolio, with a 100% performance rate as of 9/30/25 and a Loan To Value ratio of 66.2%:

The West remains TRTX's biggest regional exposure, with California its largest lending state, at 28%, plus 6% in Arizona and 5% in Texas. The Northeast is comprised of NY, at 14%, and NJ, at 5%. The Southeast contains Florida, at 9%:

Management moved into more Multi-Family loans in 2024, which were 52% of the portfolio as of 12/31/24, vs. 49.2% in 2023. This category decreased to 51.6% in Q3 '25.

The biggest change over the past year has been the increase in Industrial exposure, from 1.1% to 12.2%, as of 9/30/25.

They also decreased Office Loans from 19.9% to 17.8% in 2024 due to the pressures of the work-at-home trend upon office properties. Office property loan commitments have decreased by over 70% since 2021 and were at 15.6% as of 9/30/25.

Earnings:

Q3 '25:

"In the third quarter, TRTX's investment activity accelerated. We closed $279 million of new investments during the quarter, another $197 million subsequent to quarter end. And beyond that, we currently have over $670 million of loans expected to close in the fourth quarter." (Q3 '25 earnings call.)

Q3 was the strongest quarter for Net Income and EPS over the past 4 quarters:

Q1-3 2025 saw lower Net Interest Income. Other Revenue, Net Income, and EBIT, due to a weak first quarter.

Common Dividends Coverage:

TRTX's management uses Distributable Earnings, which adds back non-cash charges, such as Depreciation and Amortization, to Net Income, as its dividend sustainability metric.

On that basis, the common dividends were slightly undercovered in full-year 2025, at .98X, and coverage declined further in Q1-3 '25, to .84X:

Preferred Dividends:

The TPG RE Finance Trust Preferred C shares (TRTX-C) are cumulative shares, meaning that TRTX can't pay any common dividends until it pays preferred shareholders for any skipped preferred dividends. This is significant, as it pays $.24 per common share quarterly and yields over 10%. We bought the preferred shares for their cumulative feature's extra security and their much higher dividend coverage ratio.

At $18.60, TRTX-C yields 8.40%—it pays $.3906/share quarterly. It also goes ex-dividend and pays on a quarterly March/June/Sept/Dec. schedule.

Preferred dividend coverage remains solid, at 5.10X for Q1-3 '25 vs. 5.07X in 2024, on a Net Income basis. Coverage was weaker on an Operating Cash Flow basis, declining to 5.95X in Q1-3 '25 vs. 7.64X in 2024. However, the OCF/Preferred Dividend coverage is also solid:

Call Potential:

Management has repurchased 3.2 million shares of common stock at a weighted average price of $7.89 per share, resulting in $0.13 per share of book value accretion in the current year.

The common shares yield over 10%, vs. 8.4% for the TRTX-C preferreds.

We may see management begin to redeem some of the 8.05M shares of TRTX-C after the 6/14/2026 call date. It'd cost the company ~$201M to redeem the entire issue. There's a big spread between the company's average 1.68% cost of funds and the 6.25% coupon rate of the TRTX-C preferreds.

While there's no maturity date for TRTX-C, there's a 6/14/26 call date, when these shares could be redeemed at $25.00.

In that event, new buyers at $18.60 would realize a capital gain of $6.40/share, plus $.78 in remaining dividends between now and then.

That would equal a 38.61% total return, or 113.6574% annualized. It would cost TRTX $175M to retire the 7M outstanding shares.

Taxes:

REIT preferred dividends do not qualify for the lower 15-20% tax rate.

Profitability & Leverage:

ROA and ROE profitability both slipped in Q1-3 '25, with ROE much lower than the Mortgage REIT industry average. EBIT/Interest coverage was stable, although much lower than average. Debt/Equity leverage rose 40 basis points, while Net Debt/Assets was roughly stable.

Debt & Liquidity:

In mid-November, management closed TRTX 2025-FL7, a $1.1 billion managed Commercial Real Estate Collateralized Loan Obligation (“CRE CLO”). The Company placed approximately $957.0 million of investment grade securities with institutional investors providing term financing on a non-mark-to-market, non-recourse basis. TRTX redeemed 2025 TRTX 2021-FL4, a CRE CLO with ~$411.5 million of investment grade securities outstanding in November '25.

As of 9/30/25, TRTX had $216.4M in liquidity, and $1.6B in Financing capacity. 87% of its Financing was non mark to market, with a 1.95% weighted average cost of funds.

TRTX's financing looks well-laddered into the future, with a low average cost of 1.68%.

Management Change:

Senior Advisor Foley will be retiring.

"Brandon Fox has assumed the role of Interim CFO, and Ryan Roberto has assumed all duties regarding capital markets and portfolio management. The succession plan was in place prior to my decision to retire, and Brandon and Ryan have been growing into their new roles for several years. The final stages of this transition will be complete by the holiday season." (Q3 '25 earnings call.)

Risks:

  • Economic Risk: A prolonged recession would pressure some borrowers' operations, and subsequently hamper loan repayments.
  • Interest Rate Risk: As detailed previously, lower rates will result in lower earnings for TRTX, but not dramatically, especially if mgt. is able to counter lower rates with higher loan volumes.
  • Counter-party Risk: Loans usually have default risk. However, TRTX's risk ratings have been stable over the past 4 quarters, ranging from 3.01 to 3.02. Management grades their investments from 1 to 5, with 1 being the highest rating, and 5 being the lowest.

TRTX's Current Expected Credit Losses, "CECL" reserve decreased by $2.6 million quarter-over-quarter, primarily due to loan repayments, while the reserve rate of 176 basis points is flat from June 30. '25:

Parting Thoughts:

When it comes to the TRTX common vs. the TRTX-C preferred, we prefer the preferred, due to their much better dividend coverage, cumulative status, and higher placement in the credit stack.

Hence, we carry these preferreds in our Hidden Dividend Stocks Plus service.

All tables furnished by Hidden Dividend Stocks Plus, unless otherwise noted.

data.title: Diversify, But Do Not Abandon Tech
data.description:
After more than three years of stellar gains, technology companies, or more specifically their stock prices, are struggling. Expectations for economic acceleration and overly concentrated portfolios have led investors to reallocate to many previously ignored parts of the market. While I believe there are interesting non-tech opportunities and some reallocation is justified. I don't think investors should abandon the sector.

It was a very good run while it lasted. Between October of 2022 and last November's market bottom, the S&P Technology sector gained roughly 130%, outperforming the market by approximately 50%. For the leaders, the gains were far larger. The semiconductor industry advanced 275%, while market leader Nvidia surged by more than 1,500%. Given the length and magnitude of these gains, a little catch-up by the rest of the market appears warranted.

Beyond bargain hunting, there are at least two factors driving the broadening out of the equity rally and the recent underperformance by technology companies. The economy is accelerating, and the earnings gap between tech and the rest is narrower than a few years ago.

Following a brief collapse in confidence last spring, economic expectations are rising. According to Bloomberg, a consensus of economic forecasts for 2026 real GDP has risen from 1.4% last May to 2.1% today. As investors discount faster growth on the back of capital spending and tax refunds, they are more inclined to rotate towards economically sensitive sectors, notably energy, materials and industrials.

The second catalyst for the rotation is a shift in earnings expectations. To be more precise, investors are now expecting a narrowing gap between mega-cap tech and the rest of the market. This marks a significant shift, as the large tech companies were one of the few bright spots during the earnings slowdown of 2023 and 2024. Today, with more sectors expected to post positive earnings growth, the differential between mega-cap tech and the rest of the market is much smaller.

However, the rush out of technology names, particularly the mega-cap variety, ignores the sector's earnings momentum. For Q4, the tech sector is expected to post approximately 25% year-over-year earnings growth, far ahead of the 8% for the broader market. Earnings momentum is also strongest in the tech sector, with by far the most positive changes in earnings expectations (see Chart 1). Finally, the superstar companies are still the big driver of earnings growth. Nvidia is expected to be the single largest contributor to this quarter's earnings growth.

U.S. sector earnings momentum

Change in 12m forward earnings estimate

Diversify don't abandon

Given the economic outlook, value differentials and the lingering impact of too much crowding, some sector reallocation makes sense. Large banks, consumer discretionary and select industrials offer leverage to what is likely to prove an accelerating economy. That said, the valuations of many mega-cap tech names have normalized, while the sector is likely to continue to deliver unusually strong earnings growth. The sector is still very much worth holding.

This post originally appeared on BlackRock.

data.title: This Bull Market Is Gaining Strength
data.description:
AI has gone from fueling greed and unbridled speculation to now fears that the new technology will undermine businesses in multiple sectors of the market. Additionally, companies building the infrastructure are raising unprecedented sums of money in credit markets at a cost that prices in very little risk. Behind it all, we have the ongoing rotation in leadership from growth to value that began in earnest last November. This combination is devastating leveraged speculators in expensive growth stocks, precious metals, and cryptocurrencies. Yesterday, it felt like the babies were being thrown out with the bathwater, as there were very few places to hide.

It may seem ironic, but this is a very healthy exercise for the market. While the doomsdayers are out in full force warning about bubbles, recessions, and bear markets, this pullback should clear the decks for the next broad market advance. There is increasing dispersion in performance between sectors, as well as within them. In other words, this is a stock picker's market. That is not a sign of weakness. In fact, I argue that the bull market is gaining strength. Look beyond what has led the past three years, and there are outsized opportunities ahead.

Today, investors are myopically focused on the companies and industries that AI will hurt, but they are forgetting about the ones that are implementing it to improve margins and profits. Many of those companies are in sectors unrelated to the tech names that are involved in the building out of AI. We saw the same dynamic with the rollout of the internet to the masses in the 1990s. The pain some investors are feeling today is from the unwinding of leverage in what has worked consistently for three years. Meanwhile, there is a “changing of the guard,” as other sectors of the market present better value propositions.

The improvement in breadth is reflective of the broadening in earnings growth across sectors and stocks, as the market is no longer as reliant on the tech sector to deliver all of the growth. This is an extremely positive development for the vitality of the bull market. The most significant development is the leadership from small caps. If the US economy was deteriorating, as those more bearish suggest, then small company stocks that are more domestically focused would not be leading this market. The Russell 2000 index is up 5.4% in 2026, while the S&P 500 is off less than 1%.

Better yet, the global economy is picking up steam, which supports companies that have international operations. Societe Generale’s Global Cycle Indicator has risen to the upper quartile of its range, indicating increasing strength across manufacturing, trade, and other financial variables. This suggests that the strength in more cyclical sectors of the market is warranted, and it can persist for some time.

The bears continue to warn that valuations are at all-time highs, risk premiums are near all-time lows, and that the AI investment boom is going to bust just like it did with the internet in 2000. They point to the market concentration in the technology sector as another warning sign. There may be some truth here, but these are horrible timing tools, as evident by the fact that these were all warning signs one and two years ago to no avail. A bull market can run for a very long time under these conditions. There needs to be a catalyst for these conditions to become relevant, and I don’t see one yet. Furthermore, there are plenty of stocks, sectors, and markets where I can invest in more value and growth at a reasonable price.

data.title: First Busey: Preferred Share Has High Yield But Overpriced
data.description:
Introduction

First Busey Corporation (BUSE) is a regional bank. While the bank has a wide presence, most of its locations are in Illinois and the St. Louis Metropolitan area. Along with its common shares, the bank also has a preferred share (BUSEP). On its face, investors may be attracted to the 7.9% dividend yield, but the current premium over call price creates a bit of a misnomer on returns and fixed income investors should wait for better pricing.

First Busey Financial Results

While most banks have seen their rates come under pressure as the Fed has eased short-term rates back, First Busey has seen the opposite. Asset yields climbed through 2024 and into 2025, peaking at 5.63% in the second quarter. After holding at that level in the third quarter, asset yields fell 10 basis points in the fourth quarter. Borrowing yields behaved similarly, peaking at 2.91% in the second quarter before dropping to 2.57% in the fourth quarter.

The changes in the asset and borrowing yields have led to the best net interest spreads for the bank since 2019. Net interest spreads rose by 19 basis points to 2.96% while net interest margin, which includes the effects of leverage, increased by 13 basis points to 3.71%. The bank’s net interest margin is at the highest level in at least six years.

One of the reasons that the bank’s rates continued to rise was likely due to their acquisition of CrossFirst Bankshares earlier in the year. In the fourth quarter, interest income declined by $10 million compared to the previous quarter. Interest expense declined by an even greater $11 million. This led to an increase in net interest income (interest income less interest expense) of $1 million to $157 million.

Changes in Loans and Deposits

First Busey’s balance sheet expanded with the acquisition of CrossFirst Bankshares with loans growing by more than 80% and deposits growing by 65%. Since the acquisition, loan growth has declined for three consecutive quarters. The fourth quarter represented the slowest contraction of loans during that time, with lending declining by just 0.2% for the quarter.

On the deposit side, First Busey saw a more rapid decline. It is typical for banks to see some deposit decline after a merger, but for First Busey, the declines came at a rate of more than 4% in back-to-back quarters. Fortunately, the decline slowed to 1.1% in the fourth quarter, but deposits are now only 49% higher than where they were before the merger.

The downward pressure on deposits has placed an upward pressure on the bank’s loan-to-deposit ratio. First Busey’s loan-to-deposit ratio was below 80% prior to the acquisition, but in the fourth quarter, it reached 90%. The loan-to-deposit ratio is important because it indicates a bank’s capacity to fund its loan growth through deposits without having to take on external debt.

While the loan-to-deposit ratio has climbed and is a tad high at 90%, there are other regional banks that have higher loan-to-deposit ratios. In fact, the bank has been able to properly fund loan growth with deposits as external financing remains under $500 million or around 3.5% of gross loans. Investors should watch the loan-to-deposit ratio carefully, as it could trigger more external financing if it drifts higher, which would impact net interest income and earnings.

Risks to First Busey

Investors should keep an eye on First Busey’s loan performance for signs of trouble, especially since the bank is only a year removed from an acquisition. While nonperforming assets jumped from under 0.2% to over 0.4% of gross loans from late 2024 into 2025, that level has held for a few quarters in a row. The allowance for credit losses is sufficient to cover nonperforming assets at just under 1.3% of gross loans.

Why Hold Off on Preferred Shares?

With a dividend yield of 7.9% in this interest rate environment, what’s not to like about the First Busey preferred share? The challenge comes in the pricing. While the yield is high, the preferred shares are priced at $26.16 or $1.16 over their call price. With a call date of June 1, 2030, should the securities be called, their yield would drop to 6.6%. This level of return is more in line with larger banks's preferred shares; therefore, I believe investors should be patient for a better entry point.

Conclusion

First Busey is looking decent after its acquisition. Despite the drag on deposit growth, the bank has still been able to grow net interest income. The higher loan to deposit ratio could put pressure on earnings, so I’m staying clear of the common shares. I do believe that the preferred shares are safe and, with a modest selloff, should start to trade above an 8% yield. When the yield to call exceeds 7%, that will be a good time for income investors to consider taking a position.

data.title: Qfin Holdings: Profitable, Cash-Rich, And Left For Dead
data.description:
Investment Thesis: The Regulatory Pivot

The prevailing narrative for the Chinese economy is that it’s in a precarious position, with well known geopolitical tensions constantly simmering beneath the surface. Macroeconomic issues abound, such as excessive price wars that create a race to the bottom, a deflating real estate market bubble, low household consumption, a soft job market (especially for the young), a declining working age population, and GDP growth of 5% for 2025.

However, this view overlooks a structural pivot, because these issues are being addressed with the upcoming 15th Five-Year Plan, and updated Anti-Unfair Competition Law (AUCL). These policy changes are geared specifically towards the stabilization and strengthening of the Chinese economy both domestically and internationally, and should theoretically benefit QFIN (QFIN), which I rate a buy.

Company Overview: From P2P Lender to "Capital Light" Platform

QFIN is a Chinese credit-tech, aka “fintech”, platform. They partner with financial institutions to assist in the borrower acquisition and loan facilitation process. What differentiates them from other players in the space is the fact that they are a spin-off of the 360 Group, which includes 360 Total Security–China’s largest cybersecurity firm. This "Security DNA" is the secret sauce to the company’s success, because they look at credit-risk scoring from a unique perspective.

Think about what a cybersecurity firm actually monitors: digital footprints. This includes specific app usage, location stability, and social connections. It’s these specific data-mining sets that QFIN’s novel AI Argus Engine has access to. For example, if a user has certain “risky” apps (gambling, adult content, multiple financial loan services) installed on their phone, it stands to reason they will be a “risky” borrower. If their location consistently shows they are in a “work” area like a city during normal hours, they most likely have steady employment. If they have social connections who have historically paid back loans on time, they are “safer.” This allows QFIN to score the cohort of borrowers who have no credit history to rely on, and those of whom financial institutions are unable to score currently due to their legacy systems which rely on purely historical, backwards looking information.

QFIN’s AI Argus Engine is used to assist financial institutions in borrower acquisition and loan facilitation via their “credit-driven services” segment, which they refer to as “Capital Heavy.” They call it Capital Heavy because they bear credit risk with these types of loans. If they are partnering with financial institutions, they generate revenue from guarantee fees (percentage of loan OR outstanding balance). When they directly fund the loans they do it through their micro loan lender Fuzhou Microcredit, ABS’s, trusts and rarely, direct balance sheet risk. Here they generate interest income and ABS spread income.

Tying it all together we have the “platform services” segment. This comprises loan facilitation and post-facilitation services under the Capital Light model, intelligent marketing services to financial institution partners under Intelligence Credit Engine "ICE", referral services and risk management SaaS.

The Capital Light model is the opposite of Capital Heavy–here they just help with borrower acquisition, scoring and loan facilitation services throughout the loan lifecycle. On these types of loans they generate revenue from the loan facilitation fees, technology solutions/SaaS fees, and servicing fees. QFIN is effectively involved in the total loan “life-cycle” from start to finish– acquisition, credit assessment, loan facilitation, and post facilitation services such as loan performance monitoring and collection.

The Macro Pivot: 15th Five-Year Plan & The End of Destructive Price Wars

In October 2025, the recommendations for the 15th Five-Year Plan, explicitly promoting "high quality growth" via the amended Anti-Unfair Competition Law, was released. While the market often fears regulation, this is a lifeline for QFIN. Amendments (Articles 14 and 15) legally prevent “below cost” pricing and predatory payment terms from larger conglomerates, which should help establish a margin floor for small and medium sized enterprises (QFIN’s core borrower).

Crucially, Article 40 introduces "extra-territoriality," allowing Beijing to penalize companies that "export deflation" abroad if it disrupts domestic market order. This signals a historic pivot from "market share at any cost" to "sustainable profit margins." Combined with Vice Premier He Lifeng’s Davos 2026 declaration of China wanting to become the “world’s market” and a "consumption powerhouse," the regulatory headwinds that once crushed fintech valuations have officially turned into tailwinds.

The Structural Shift: From Real Estate to "New Consumption"

With the structural decline of the real estate market well-documented, the 15th Five-Year Plan has pivoted policy from "new builds" to "trade-ins" and renovation. This is a direct catalyst for QFIN. Instead of financing risky down payments for speculative properties, QFIN is now financing the safer, smaller-ticket "facelift,” e.g., new floors, appliances, and home decor for properties users already own. This trend is supported by the government’s front-loaded 62.5B Yuan (~$9 Billion) subsidy for consumer goods trade-ins. There is recent evidence of its success, as in 2025 the consumer goods trade-in policy led to record highs in sales of home appliances and communication devices.

With real estate losing its status as a store of value and bank deposit rates near 1%, Chinese households are sitting on ~$23 Trillion in deposits searching for yield. This should create a demand shift toward dividend-paying equities. QFIN, with its aggressive buybacks and high dividend yield (currently ~9%), sits in the "Goldilocks" zone because they offer a better yield than bank deposits without the volatility of pure growth stocks.

The Chinese government recognizes this, and at the beginning of 2025 began to take steps to attract medium and long term investments into their capital market, specifically from commercial insurance, national social security and basic pension funds.

This push is gaining traction. In 2025, assets in China’s wealth management sector rose to 33.29 trillion yuan (~$4.76 Trillion), marking an increase of 11.15% from the start of the period. Investor confidence in the sector and products is increasing as well, with 143 million individuals holding wealth management products by year end, which is an increase of 14.37%.

The Demographic Opportunity: Why "Youth Unemployment" is a Bull Signal

The final piece of the macro puzzle is the most misunderstood: China's demographic decline. Investors believe that a shrinking workforce is a death sentence for growth; but it’s actually a driver of wage inflation. As labor becomes scarcer, it becomes more expensive, leading to higher disposable income for the working class. In the long term this will create a wealthier, more resilient borrower base for QFIN.

According to December 2025 reports, youth unemployment has been declining, and the overall unemployment rate sits at 5.1%, which is unchanged MoM and remains the lowest level in 4 months.

This youth cohort is not "unemployable"; they are educated graduates waiting for white-collar tech roles. In the interim, they work in the gig economy (services), creating a massive demographic of "Asset-Light, Cash-Flow Positive" borrowers who need credit for lifestyle and upskilling. This is why traditional banks hate them and QFIN loves them: they don’t have tangible assets or credit history with which banks can vet them, but QFIN does, with their AI powered Argus Engine

QFIN’s Argus Engine utilizes device-level footprints (e.g., location stability, app usage patterns) to score borrowers with no credit history. While traditional banks rely on backward-looking credit bureaus, Argus uses real-time behavioral data to predict repayment probability, allowing QFIN to safely lend to the “unscoreable” gig-economy worker that banks reject.

The Tech Moat: "Argus" & The Bank Partnership Pivot

China’s consumer credit card industry is currently undergoing a major transformation due to younger consumers' preferences for online lenders and simpler digital alternatives. Traditional banks aren’t good at this because they are rigid, require physical verification, complex approvals, and have high fixed costs like annual fees. Younger borrowers don’t want to deal with this, so instead they are moving to the simpler, more streamlined “tech-first credit.”

QFIN is perfect for this. They are a digital online lender who has superior ability to score these types of borrowers, and legacy banks need them in order to upgrade their lending practices and software to capture this market share. Argus rates a potential borrower's credit, and the "ICE" (Intelligent Credit Engine) can deliver these borrowers to the banks because QFIN’s software is “sticky” and sits inside all of the consumer spending apps and websites.

This partnership model is validating in real-time. In Q3 2025, CEO Haisheng Wu confirmed that the Technology Solutions business (the “capital light” segment) saw loan volume explode by 218% sequentially. This was achieved by partnering with over 20 financial institutions who are now integrating QFIN’s “AI Agents” deeply into their own ecosystems, effectively transforming QFIN from a simple vendor into a critical infrastructure provider. They are positioning themselves as the “China FICO and AWS for credit.” If these banks outsource their risk models, QFIN’s moat explodes.

Also recall that “AI development and transformation” is a main theme of the 15th FYP with the “AI+ Initiative,” which mandates the integration of AI into the “Real Economy.” This formally categorizes AI not just as a “tech sector” but a “New Productive Force,” and that the government views AI as the engine that will help upgrade traditional industries like manufacturing and finance. This is the regulatory tailwind that benefits companies like QFIN, because their business model is literally "AI + Finance.”

Institutional Validation: Why Smart Money is Buying the Dip

I’m not going to sugarcoat it, the one year TTM chart looks terrible; nothing but a falling knife.

But despite the ugly one year chart, if we look at the one month chart, the price seems to be stabilizing, with a recent spike on 2/6/26, and a "Whale" appears to be absorbing the panic selling.

While the price has drifted lower recently, as of 2/6/2026, the On-Balance Volume (OBV) and Accumulation/Distribution (A/D) lines have diverged aggressively upward for almost two weeks. This is a massive anomaly. It signals that while retail investors are selling the "bid," large institutional buyers are hitting the "ask."

My theory for this accumulation is simple: At ~$15/share, QFIN trades at an Enterprise Value of just ~$1.47B. When you compare this to their projected ~$840M midpoint 2025 net income, the market is effectively valuing this high-growth fintech at 1.75x EV/Earnings. This is a distressed valuation for a profitable, cash-rich operating business.

Even if you stress-test 2025 earnings by cutting them in half (~$429M), a buyer at these levels would achieve a full payback in ~3.5 years.

Concerns about "fake cash" are refuted by the sheer scale of capital returns. In 2025 alone, QFIN returned ~$627M (dividends + buybacks), representing a 73% payout ratio. You cannot fake a wire transfer to shareholders. They are begging you to invest with their shareholder friendly returns.

The Paper Trail of Insider Buying

QFIN announced on their Q3 2025 earnings call that they had another ~$170M left under their announced $450M 2025 buyback program, and that they would resume repurchases immediately following the call.

Hongyi Zhou, the founder of the company, made a significant open market buy on December 5, 2025, according to an SEC 13D/A filing. On that date he purchased ~850K shares right after the “disastrous” Q3 earnings and guidance, increasing his stake in the company to 17.9%.

Last, we have the “Smart Money” signal from Oversea-Chinese Banking Corp Ltd (OCBC). They already filed their Q4 2025 13F and during this period they registered a ~742% increase in their QFIN holding.

OCBC, Southeast Asia’s second largest bank, is a conservative lender–not a hype-chasing hedge fund. Institutions generally have strict due-diligence protocols they must follow regarding asset quality before establishing a position. As a Singapore-based entity with regional proximity to mainland capital flows, their investment offers a tangible counter-narrative to the broader market skepticism regarding Chinese credit.

Valuation:

I am going to use a conservative forward looking valuation. Due to the concern over the 20% APR cap, I have attempted to adjust for this by extrapolating the 2026 figures from Q3 2025 earnings (where the average APR 20.9%). To do this I removed the excess .9% interest from Q3 2025’s average outstanding loan balance to model a fully compliant, lower-rate environment.

The final price target is a 50/50 blend of RIM and DCF models. This mirrors the company’s projected 2026 loan structure split of capital light vs capital heavy, as per CFO Zuoli Xu. Accordingly, I value half the firm as a lender and half as a tech platform.

GAAP Net income: ~$700M (Adjusted for 20% APR cap)

Cost of Equity: 14–15% (Incorporating a China Risk Premium)

Net Debt: ~($562M)

Shares Outstanding (Treasury Stock Method): 132M (ADR)

Tangible Book Value: $25.06

Terminal Growth: All valuations will be based on the most conservative 0% terminal growth scenario.

In the 100% payout ratio scenario we get a price of ~$36, which is a ~58% current discount to fair value.

Next is the punitive 100% retention ratio capital allocation scenario.

Even in this scenario we still get a ~$20 price, which is a current ~25% discount to fair value.

FCFF: $1.10B (Adjusted for 20% APR cap)

All Other Inputs Same as Above

Here we get a ~$55 price, which is a current ~72% discount to fair value.

RIM Valuation: ~$20 (using the most punitive 100% retention ratio valuation)

DCF Valuation: ~$55

50/50 Blended Target: $20 x 50% + $55 x 50% = ~$38

What is the downside? Historically, distressed Chinese financials trade at ~2x Earnings. QFIN is already trading at ~2.3x Earnings. The market has priced in the disaster. The downside is anchored by the massive cash pile ($1.5B), while the upside is uncapped by the policy pivot.

Risks: The "Darwinian" Bear Case

The main risk that is currently a huge overhang for QFIN is the fear of the regulatory 20% APR Cap Rate. The bearish view is that capping interest rates will crush margins, but I believe that the cap is a moat; let’s call it the “Darwinian Consolidation.”

The Chinese fintech market, historically, has been fragmented. It was filled with thousands of small, inefficient “loan facilitators” who relied on two things to survive; usurious rates of 36%+ APR to cover massive default rates and regulatory arbitrage, lending with almost 0% “skin in the game.”

The new regulatory framework kills both: with the hard legal cap at 24% (and regulators effectively pushing the industry standard to under 20%), smaller players with high customer acquisition costs simply cannot turn a profit. They are mathematically insolvent.

We also have the "30% Rule." This rule was originally introduced by the CBIRC in 2021 and requires platforms to fund 30% of every joint loan. While major players complied years ago, the new 2025 Anti-Unfair Competition Law is now aggressively purging smaller, non-compliant platforms that were using loopholes to survive. The era of “regulatory arbitrage” is officially over.

This all leads to QFIN being one of the “Last Men Standing” in the fintech world, making this a classic “industry consolidation” play.

Remember, QFIN’s “capital Light” "ICE" model bypasses the capital-heavy requirements because it acts purely as a tech enabler for banks, not a co-lender. As the government forces the "zombie lenders" to exit, their millions of customers don't disappear—they migrate. They move to the few licensed, tech-efficient platforms that can profitably lend at sub-20% rates. Far from a threat, the rate cap is effectively handing QFIN the market share of its dying competitors on a silver platter.

Then there’s the ever-present geo-political risks, mainly between China and the US. If the trade war rhetoric flares up again in the coming months, this could obviously harm Chinese stocks.

And if the Chinese economy is truly in the middle of an “economic collapse,” as has been predicted by Western media outlets, then loan defaults will rise. But QFIN has fortress level protection against this, though. As stated in their Q3 2025 earnings release and call, they have increased their provision coverage ratio to 613%–6x the capital needed to cover its current bad loans. This suggests that even if defaults tripled overnight, the company’s balance sheet would remain solvent. This is not a fragile lender; it is an over-reserved fortress.

Another concern would be the dividend/buyback policy going forward. If QFIN’s net income margin drops by 50% or more (something which I don’t think anyone is currently predicting) then their capital allocation strategy could change. While they acknowledged in their Q3 2025 earnings call that they plan to maintain the 20%-30% dividend payout ratio going forward, even with the volatile earnings movement, they did state that “the mix between the buyback and dividend payout may change from time to time, depending on the situation that we are facing at any given time.” This leads me to believe that the dividend payout ratio will stay around for the foreseeable future, as the cash flow payout ratio for the current year is ~20%; it’s the buybacks that I believe would be at risk.

Last is that which may be the most important of all: Ideology. There are a lot of investors who dislike China for many reasons—ideological, political, economical. This creates risk aversion to Chinese stocks as a whole. Since these investors help to “make a market,” just like myself, and if they have a negative view of China and choose not to invest, then it’s very possible that any Chinese stock, including QFIN, will never achieve its “fair value.” It doesn’t matter how fundamentally sound, safe and profitable a company may be, people just do not trust it enough to invest.

Conclusion: Asymmetric Upside

I consider myself to be a contrarian investor. I believe the best opportunities lie in unloved, overlooked, and left for dead companies. QFIN is currently one of them. This investment is currently not for the faint of heart, as the volatility has been higher than its historical average over the last year, and its chart looks terrible.

According to certain sources, the “Chinese economic collapse” has already occurred, and there is no way forward for them in the current geo-political framework.

I am currently taking the other side of that bet. The 15th FYP prioritizes AI and domestic consumption—policy goals that strongly align with QFIN's business model.

I believe QFIN at its current single digit historical “distressed” financial company PE level of ~2x presents an asymmetric risk/reward setup scenario. Specific metrics that should help any investor feel safe in the fundamentals of QFIN are the $1.5B cash on hand, 24.1% buyback-adjusted ROE, 613% provision ratio, and the 218% technology solutions growth. For all these reasons I rate QFIN a Buy.
